bins.name: Protecting existing customers from cost-shifting
bins.desc: Southern Companies say they integrate large loads while protecting existing customers from cost-shifting, using minimum bills, collateral, and termination protection. They argue this approach can actually benefit existing customers by spreading fixed-cost recovery across a broader base.
quotes: Southern Companies re-emphasize their support for timely and cost-effective integration of large-load customers to support growth and enable economic development and bolster the nation’s national security, in a way that protects existing customers, as called for in the Secretary of Energy’s Advanced Notice of Proposed Rulemaking

Southern Companies are connecting large load retail customers quickly and efficiently, while protecting customers from cost-shifting, as identified in the ANOPR and the President’s Ratepayer Protection Pledge

Georgia Power Company has implemented several mechanisms aimed to protect existing customers, including minimum bill obligations for large loads, long-term contractual commitments, collateral requirements, termination protection, and regulatory review and accountability for customer-specific arrangements.

Southern Companies have integrated large volumes of new loads in a manner that not only protects existing customers from additional costs, but actually benefits these existing customers through allocating fixed-cost recovery across a broader customer base.

bins.name: Existing-customer protection mechanisms
bins.desc: The filing details state-approved mechanisms that shield existing customers from large-load costs, including minimum bill obligations, collateral requirements, and termination protection. It frames these as proven safeguards against subsidization.
quotes: Southern Companies are connecting large load retail customers quickly and efficiently, while protecting customers from cost-shifting, as identified in the ANOPR and the President’s Ratepayer Protection Pledge

Georgia Power Company has implemented several mechanisms aimed to protect existing customers, including minimum bill obligations for large loads, long-term contractual commitments, collateral requirements, termination protection, and regulatory review and accountability for customer-specific arrangements.

Southern Companies have integrated large volumes of new loads in a manner that not only protects existing customers from additional costs, but actually benefits these existing customers through allocating fixed-cost recovery across a broader customer base.

bins.name: Collateral and contract commitments
bins.desc: Southern Companies cite collateral requirements and long-term contractual commitments among the tools that protect existing customers from large-load risk.
quotes: Georgia Power Company has implemented several mechanisms aimed to protect existing customers, including minimum bill obligations for large loads, long-term contractual commitments, collateral requirements, termination protection, and regulatory review and accountability for customer-specific arrangements.

bins.name: Preserve state retail jurisdiction
bins.desc: Southern Companies ask the Commission to preserve the historic jurisdiction of state regulators over retail large-load connections, whose terms are embedded in bundled rate mechanisms. They want federal action to defer to well-functioning state-led frameworks.
quotes: Southern Companies respectfully request that the Commission allow Southern Companies to continue building on this record of success and progress towards the ANOPR’s goals, while preserving the historic jurisdiction of our state regulators over retail customer connections, the terms and conditions of which are inextricably embedded in our bundled rate mechanisms.
We respectfully suggest that any federal initiative that comes from this proceeding preserve well-functioning state-led frameworks that can help deliver speed and affordability.

Any federal reforms adopted in this proceeding should preserve the flexibility for states and vertically integrated utilities to continue to use coordinated bundled-service structures that are already delivering timely infrastructure development, customer protections, and reliable service.

To the extent the Commission determines that additional near-term federal reforms are appropriate, those reforms should focus principally on regions where wholesale market structures, transmission service constructs, and Commission-jurisdictional processes are directly implicated.

bins.name: Integrated resource planning model
bins.desc: The filing credits its vertically-integrated structure and integrated resource planning for holistically combining generation, fuel, transmission, and distribution to serve large loads.
quotes: Southern Companies explained that their vertically-integrated structure and integrated resource planning process enable them to plan for and serve large load customers by holistically combining generation, fuel, transmission, and distribution planning.

bins.name: Preserve flexibility, no uniform rule
bins.desc: Southern Companies argue regional differences justify preserving flexibility for frameworks already delivering timely infrastructure, and that a one-size-fits-all rule would be counterproductive. They suggest any near-term reforms focus on regions where wholesale markets and Commission-jurisdictional processes are directly implicated.
quotes: Southern Companies’ experience demonstrates that coordinated state-regulated planning structures can support more timely generation development and reduce interconnection delays relative to regions currently experiencing substantial queue backlogs and infrastructure constraints.  These differing regional conditions support preserving flexibility for frameworks already providing coordinated and timely infrastructure development.
We respectfully suggest that any federal initiative that comes from this proceeding preserve well-functioning state-led frameworks that can help deliver speed and affordability.

Given the success of state-regulated integrated resource planning and bundled retail service frameworks in integrating large load customers, such as in Southern Companies’ territories, a one-size-fits-all rule would be counterproductive.

Any federal reforms adopted in this proceeding should preserve the flexibility for states and vertically integrated utilities to continue to use coordinated bundled-service structures that are already delivering timely infrastructure development, customer protections, and reliable service.

To the extent the Commission determines that additional near-term federal reforms are appropriate, those reforms should focus principally on regions where wholesale market structures, transmission service constructs, and Commission-jurisdictional processes are directly implicated.

bins.name: Targeted near-term reforms only
bins.desc: Southern Companies accept that targeted near-term reforms may be appropriate, but only for RTO/market regions, not for state-regulated vertically integrated territories.
quotes: To the extent the Commission determines that additional near-term federal reforms are appropriate, those reforms should focus principally on regions where wholesale market structures, transmission service constructs, and Commission-jurisdictional processes are directly implicated.

bins.name: Speed to power for large loads
bins.desc: The filing emphasizes connecting large loads quickly and efficiently as a shared goal aligned with the ANOPR and the Ratepayer Protection Pledge.
quotes: Southern Companies re-emphasize their support for timely and cost-effective integration of large-load customers to support growth and enable economic development and bolster the nation’s national security, in a way that protects existing customers, as called for in the Secretary of Energy’s Advanced Notice of Proposed Rulemaking

bins.name: Bundled retail service authority
bins.desc: Southern Companies stress that retail connection terms are inextricably embedded in their bundled rate mechanisms under state oversight, which they want preserved.
quotes: Southern Companies respectfully request that the Commission allow Southern Companies to continue building on this record of success and progress towards the ANOPR’s goals, while preserving the historic jurisdiction of our state regulators over retail customer connections, the terms and conditions of which are inextricably embedded in our bundled rate mechanisms.

bins.name: Updated large-load success data
bins.desc: The filing supplements the record with current figures, including 32 large-load customers committing to about 15,600 MW and 9,572 MW under executed contracts, to show its model is working.
quotes: As of April 2026, 32 large load customers have committed to receive approximately 15,600 MW of electric service from Georgia Power, including executed Contracts for Electric Service for 9,572 MW of large load.

bins.name: State-led integrated planning benefits
bins.desc: Southern Companies argue coordinated state-regulated planning supports timely generation development and reduces interconnection delays compared with regions facing queue backlogs.
quotes: Southern Companies explained that their vertically-integrated structure and integrated resource planning process enable them to plan for and serve large load customers by holistically combining generation, fuel, transmission, and distribution planning.

Southern Companies’ experience demonstrates that coordinated state-regulated planning structures can support more timely generation development and reduce interconnection delays relative to regions currently experiencing substantial queue backlogs and infrastructure constraints.  These differing regional conditions support preserving flexibility for frameworks already providing coordinated and timely infrastructure development.
